title: Azure ML Platform IaC - Project Case Study
content: Title: Azure ML Platform IaC - Project Case Study Source URL: https://rajesharigala.com/mlops/ai2/ai2.1 Headings: H1: Azure ML Platform Infrastructure as Code H2: Project Summary H2: Key Technologies & Concepts H2: Problem & Objective H2: Solution & Architecture H2: AI/ML & DevOps Details H2: Skills & Technologies Used H2: Challenges & Outcomes H2: Azure DevOps CI/CD - Architecture & YAML Mapping H2: Assets & References H3: Project Category H3: Industry/Domain H3: AI/ML/DevOps Focus H3: Azure ML Platform IaC Keywords H3: Problems Solved H3: Primary Objectives H3: Solution Overview H3: Key Components H3: AI/ML Type or DevOps Focus H3: Models, Pipeline, or Automation H3: CI/CD & Containerization Tools H3: Monitoring & Optimization H3: Primary Skills H3: Secondary Tools / Frameworks H3: Programming Languages H3: Cloud & DevOps Tools H3: Key Technical Challenges H3: How They Were Resolved H3: Flow H3: PIPELINE-1: Deploy Infrastructure H3: GitHub Repository H3: PIPELINE-1: GitHub H3: Study Material Resources H3: Study Material - Azure ML Platform IaC Paragraphs: Production-grade End-to-end Azure ML platform automation with IaC Production-grade End-to-end Azure ML platform infrastructure automation provisioned using Infrastructure as Code with secure RBAC and autoscaling compute, using Azure DevOps CI/CD and Azure CLI v2. A fully automated, reproducible Azure ML platform with standardized workspace setup, secure RBAC, and cost-aware compute provisioning. Comprehensive Project Overview Cloud + MLOps (Infrastructure as Code for AI Platforms) Cross-Industry (Enterprise AI Platforms / MLOps Infrastructure) AI Platform Engineering / MLOps Infrastructure Core Technologies Used What problem did this project solve? Architectural Overview Designed and implemented a CI/CD-driven Infrastructure-as-Code pipeline that provisions and configures the complete Azure Machine Learning platform—resource groups, AML workspace, CLI context, RBAC, and autoscaling compute—using Azure DevOps YAML and Azure CLI v2. The solution creates a standardized, re-runnable cloud ML foundation that downstream training and deployment pipelines can reliably build upon. The platform is designed to scale compute elastically using Azure ML autoscaling clusters (min=0, max=N) to handle variable training loads while controlling cost by provisioning low priority instances during training. (This approach can reduce training costs but is the worst idea for endpoint allocation — DEDICATED MACHINES vs. LOW PRIORITY INSTANCES.) Infrastructure pipelines are idempotent and re-runnable, ensuring reliable provisioning across environments without drift. Technical Implementation Details DevOps / MLOps Platform Engineering (Infrastructure as Code for AI Platforms) CI/CD Infrastructure pipelines (Pipeline-as-Code) for automated Azure ML platform provisioning and configuration Azure DevOps (YAML Pipelines), Azure CLI v2, Azure ML CLI v2 Pipeline execution logging (Azure DevOps), Azure ML workspace telemetry, idempotent infra checks, and cost optimization via autoscaling compute (scale-to-zero) Technical Proficiency Demonstrated Technical challenges and resolutions Architecture to YAML construct mapping FLOW: Understand the flow: Subscriptions → Azure Resource Group → Name Spaces → Resources Link: Repository Code, diagrams, study material Source code repository containing the Azure ML IaC configuration and Azure DevOps pipelines. Pipeline repository for the project (Azure MLOps End-to-End). A fully automated, reproducible Azure ML platform with standardized workspace setup, secure RBAC, and cost-aware compute provisioning. Problems Solved Teams struggle to set up Azure ML environments reliably and repeatedly Infrastructure created manually leads to inconsistency across environments Poor governance, weak access control, and no cost discipline Results in broken ML pipelines, environment drift, security risks Slow onboarding of new AI projects due to manual cloud setup Primary Objectives Create a reproducible, secure, and cost-governed Azure ML platform foundation Provision on demand via CI/CD pipelines Enable consistent environments for ML training and deployment Support multiple environments (Dev/Prod) without manual cloud setup Allow ML teams to focus on models instead of cloud plumbing Solution & Architecture Architectural Overview Solution Overview Designed and implemented a CI/CD-driven Infrastructure-as-Code pipeline that provisions and configures the complete Azure Machine Learning platform—resource groups, AML workspace, CLI context, RBAC, and autoscaling compute—using Azure DevOps YAML and Azure CLI v2.

title: Azure ML Platform IaC - Project Case Study
content: Problems Solved Teams struggle to set up Azure ML environments reliably and repeatedly Infrastructure created manually leads to inconsistency across environments Poor governance, weak access control, and no cost discipline Results in broken ML pipelines, environment drift, security risks Slow onboarding of new AI projects due to manual cloud setup Primary Objectives Create a reproducible, secure, and cost-governed Azure ML platform foundation Provision on demand via CI/CD pipelines Enable consistent environments for ML training and deployment Support multiple environments (Dev/Prod) without manual cloud setup Allow ML teams to focus on models instead of cloud plumbing Solution & Architecture Architectural Overview Solution Overview Designed and implemented a CI/CD-driven Infrastructure-as-Code pipeline that provisions and configures the complete Azure Machine Learning platform—resource groups, AML workspace, CLI context, RBAC, and autoscaling compute—using Azure DevOps YAML and Azure CLI v2. The solution creates a standardized, re-runnable cloud ML foundation that downstream training and deployment pipelines can reliably build upon. The platform is designed to scale compute elastically using Azure ML autoscaling clusters (min=0, max=N) to handle variable training loads while controlling cost by provisioning low priority instances during training. (This approach can reduce training costs but is the worst idea for endpoint allocation — DEDICATED MACHINES vs. LOW PRIORITY INSTANCES.) Infrastructure pipelines are idempotent and re-runnable, ensuring reliable provisioning across environments without drift. 1 Azure Subscription 2 Azure DevOps 3 IaC Pipeline 4 Azure ML Platform 5 Secure RBAC & Compute Key Components Cloud Platform: Microsoft Azure (Azure Machine Learning, Azure DevOps, Azure Resource Manager) Services: Azure DevOps (Repos, YAML Pipelines), Azure Machine Learning (Workspace, Compute Clusters, Model Registry) Tools: Azure CLI v2, Azure ML CLI v2, Service Principals & RBAC (Contributor role) Storage & Monitoring: Azure Storage (Workspace-backed), Azure Monitor / Application Insights Instance Choice: Choosing between dedicated instances and low-priority (spot/preemptible) instances — prefer dedicated instances for stable endpoints; low-priority instances are acceptable for training to reduce cost. Creates Azure resources (Subscriptions, Resource Group, Namespaces, resources) Pipeline-2: Deploy Model Training - Executes the ML training pipeline with data prep, training, evaluation, and registration Detailed Flow Subscription & Identity: Create subscription-level RBAC roles (Contributor, Owner, Reader) and provision a Service Principal for CI/CD access to the cloud tenant. DevOps Organization: Create DevOps Org → Project → configure Project Settings with Service Connection to the cloud (Service Principal handshake). Repository Setup: Create repo (GitHub or Azure Repos) → enforce branch protection, set repo permissions (contribute, reviewers), add `config_infra.yml` and pipeline YAML files. Pipeline Security: Configure Pipeline Settings → service connections, environment approvals, and security to control who can edit build/release pipelines. Idempotent Provisioning: Pipelines run `deploy-infra.yaml` which checks/creates Resource Groups, Namespaces, Workspaces and sets CLI defaults — designed to be idempotent and re-runnable. PIPELINE-1: Deploy Infrastructure (Detailed Steps) Repos → add/edit `config_infra.yml` (namespace, workspace name, postfix, region, sizing) → commit to infra branch. Pipeline triggers on PR/merge to main or manual run for environment bootstrapping; pipeline uses Service Connection to authenticate. Post-deploy steps: output workspace connection info, store resource IDs in pipeline artifacts, and create a verification job to run quick smoke tests.

title: Enterprise AI Platform on Azure | End-to-End Azure MLOps Pipelines
content: Title: Enterprise AI Platform on Azure | End-to-End Azure MLOps Pipelines Source URL: https://rajesharigala.com/ai-work/azure-mlops-platform Headings: H1: Enterprise AI Platform on Azure H2: Project Summary H2: Business Problem H2: What The Project Does H2: Business Use Cases H2: Business Value H2: Business Architecture Story H2: Detailed Project Modules H2: Cost-Aware Azure MLOps Architecture H2: Resources the Code Uses H3: What The Project Does H3: Production Workflow H3: Reusable Platform H3: The Problem H3: The Solution H3: Infrastructure and Setup H3: Training and Governance H3: Serving and Testing H3: Cross-Industry Applications H3: Value Delivered H3: Operational Advantages H3: Architecture Overview H3: Platform Foundation (IaC - Azure ML) H3: ML TrainingPipeline H3: Production Deployment & Ops H3: Cost-Aware Design H3: Cost Considerations Implemented H3: Estimated Cost Profile H3: Resource Inventory H3: Main Cost Drivers H3: Estimated Monthly Cost Paragraphs: End-to-End Azure MLOps Pipelines for Production ML Deployment This project demonstrates an end-to-end MLOps workflow on Azure for automating machine learning model training, evaluation, registration, and deployment. Using taxi fare prediction as the sample business scenario, the solution shows how organizations can convert raw business data into a production-ready prediction service through Azure Machine Learning and Azure DevOps pipelines. While the model predicts taxi fares, the real value of the project is the reusable MLOps platform behind it. The same architecture can be adapted for real-world use cases such as demand forecasting, price prediction, customer churn prediction, risk scoring, and operational decision automation. Business-first view of the Azure MLOps platform The sample model predicts taxi fare cost using trip details such as distance, pickup and drop-off location, trip time, passenger count, vendor, and trip metadata. The broader platform automates data registration, preparation, training, evaluation, model registration, deployment, and endpoint testing through Azure DevOps pipelines. The real value is the reusable MLOps platform pattern, which can be adapted across business domains beyond taxi fare prediction. Why enterprises need a repeatable ML delivery pipeline Many companies build machine learning models in notebooks, but struggle to move them into production. Models are often trained manually, deployed inconsistently, and difficult to monitor or update. This creates delays, quality issues, poor governance, and operational risk. This project solves that problem by creating a repeatable ML delivery pipeline where data preparation, model training, validation, registration, deployment, and testing are automated. Full Azure ML workflow delivered through CI/CD Reusable architecture across multiple industries Key outcomes for delivery teams and business stakeholders How business data becomes a production prediction service A business team provides historical data. The data science pipeline prepares the data, trains the model, evaluates quality, and registers the approved model. The DevOps pipeline then deploys the model as an endpoint that applications or business systems can call for predictions. The architecture separates infrastructure, training, and deployment into independent Azure DevOps pipelines. Business data is registered in Azure ML, processed through a training pipeline, evaluated, versioned in the model registry, and then deployed through either real-time online endpoints or lower-cost batch inference depending on the business use case. Technical deep-dive pages linked from the business-first overview Infrastructure-as-code, Azure ML workspace setup, compute foundation, and Azure DevOps integration. Open module 01 Dataset registration, data preparation, training, evaluation, and model registration workflow. Open module 02 Online deployment, endpoint testing, and operational deployment patterns for production ML. Open module 03 FinOps thinking built into training, deployment, and infrastructure choices This project was designed with cloud cost control in mind. The architecture separates training, deployment, and infrastructure pipelines so compute resources are only used when required, while production endpoints can be managed based on real business demand. The training compute cluster uses a scale-to-zero configuration, meaning no training compute cost is incurred when the pipeline is idle. This makes the solution suitable for experimentation, scheduled retraining, and cost-controlled proof-of-concept deployments. For production inference, the project supports both online and batch deployment patterns. Online endpoints are suitable for real-time API predictions but have a continuous running cost.

title: Enterprise AI Platform on Azure | End-to-End Azure MLOps Pipelines
content: Open module 03 FinOps thinking built into training, deployment, and infrastructure choices This project was designed with cloud cost control in mind. The architecture separates training, deployment, and infrastructure pipelines so compute resources are only used when required, while production endpoints can be managed based on real business demand. The training compute cluster uses a scale-to-zero configuration, meaning no training compute cost is incurred when the pipeline is idle. This makes the solution suitable for experimentation, scheduled retraining, and cost-controlled proof-of-concept deployments. For production inference, the project supports both online and batch deployment patterns. Online endpoints are suitable for real-time API predictions but have a continuous running cost. Batch endpoints are more cost-effective for scheduled or offline scoring workloads where real-time response is not required. For low-volume or scheduled prediction workloads, batch inference can be used to reduce cost. For real-time business applications, the online endpoint can be enabled with a clear understanding of the monthly compute cost. Core services, resource settings, and main cost drivers The biggest cost is the online endpoint because the deployment uses STANDARD_D2AS_V4 with instance_count: 1. If this runs 24/7, estimate around $70/month for VM compute alone before storage, logs, registry, and workspace support services. Training compute is cost-controlled because the cluster is configured as training_target_min_nodes: 0, training_target_max_nodes: 4, and training_target_sku: Standard_D2s_v3. That means the training cluster can scale down to zero when idle. For a portfolio/demo project, keep the endpoint deleted when not testing. Then the monthly cost should stay around $5-$40. For a small production setup, a realistic range is often $200-$500/month depending on traffic, uptime, monitoring, and retraining frequency. Main Content: Enterprise AI Platform on Azure End-to-End Azure MLOps Pipelines for Production ML Deployment This project demonstrates an end-to-end MLOps workflow on Azure for automating machine learning model training, evaluation, registration, and deployment. Using taxi fare prediction as the sample business scenario, the solution shows how organizations can convert raw business data into a production-ready prediction service through Azure Machine Learning and Azure DevOps pipelines. While the model predicts taxi fares, the real value of the project is the reusable MLOps platform behind it. The same architecture can be adapted for real-world use cases such as demand forecasting, price prediction, customer churn prediction, risk scoring, and operational decision automation. View GitHub Repository Endpoint Link Only During Live Execution Business Problem FinOps Section Resources Section Project Summary Business-first view of the Azure MLOps platform What The Project Does The sample model predicts taxi fare cost using trip details such as distance, pickup and drop-off location, trip time, passenger count, vendor, and trip metadata. Production Workflow The broader platform automates data registration, preparation, training, evaluation, model registration, deployment, and endpoint testing through Azure DevOps pipelines. Reusable Platform The real value is the reusable MLOps platform pattern, which can be adapted across business domains beyond taxi fare prediction. Business Problem Why enterprises need a repeatable ML delivery pipeline The Problem Many companies build machine learning models in notebooks, but struggle to move them into production. Models are often trained manually, deployed inconsistently, and difficult to monitor or update. This creates delays, quality issues, poor governance, and operational risk. The Solution This project solves that problem by creating a repeatable ML delivery pipeline where data preparation, model training, validation, registration, deployment, and testing are automated. What The Project Does Full Azure ML workflow delivered through CI/CD Infrastructure and Setup Creates Azure ML infrastructure Creates compute for model training Registers training data Prepares and splits the dataset Training and Governance Trains a Random Forest regression model Evaluates model performance Registers the model after evaluation Uses Azure DevOps pipelines for CI/CD automation Serving and Testing Deploys the model to an Azure ML online endpoint Supports batch endpoint configuration Tests the deployed endpoint Supports production-style ML delivery Business Use Cases Reusable architecture across multiple industries Cross-Industry Applications Transportation: fare prediction, ride ETA, demand forecasting, driver allocation Retail: sales forecasting, price recommendation, inventory demand prediction Finance: loan risk scoring, fraud detection, customer churn prediction Healthcare: appointment no-show prediction, patient risk scoring Manufacturing: equipment failure prediction, quality inspection prediction Customer Operations: support ticket prioritization, churn prediction, next-best action Business Value Key outcomes for delivery teams and business stakeholders Value Delivered Faster model deployment Lower manual engineering effort Repeatable training and deployment process Better governance through model registration Operational Advantages Easier rollback and versioning using registered models Clear separation of infrastructure, training, and deployment pipelines Support for both real-time and batch prediction patterns Cloud-native design using Azure Machine Learning Business Architecture Story How business data becomes a production prediction service Architecture Overview A business team provides historical data.

title: Enterprise AI Platform on Azure | End-to-End Azure MLOps Pipelines
content: What The Project Does Full Azure ML workflow delivered through CI/CD Infrastructure and Setup Creates Azure ML infrastructure Creates compute for model training Registers training data Prepares and splits the dataset Training and Governance Trains a Random Forest regression model Evaluates model performance Registers the model after evaluation Uses Azure DevOps pipelines for CI/CD automation Serving and Testing Deploys the model to an Azure ML online endpoint Supports batch endpoint configuration Tests the deployed endpoint Supports production-style ML delivery Business Use Cases Reusable architecture across multiple industries Cross-Industry Applications Transportation: fare prediction, ride ETA, demand forecasting, driver allocation Retail: sales forecasting, price recommendation, inventory demand prediction Finance: loan risk scoring, fraud detection, customer churn prediction Healthcare: appointment no-show prediction, patient risk scoring Manufacturing: equipment failure prediction, quality inspection prediction Customer Operations: support ticket prioritization, churn prediction, next-best action Business Value Key outcomes for delivery teams and business stakeholders Value Delivered Faster model deployment Lower manual engineering effort Repeatable training and deployment process Better governance through model registration Operational Advantages Easier rollback and versioning using registered models Clear separation of infrastructure, training, and deployment pipelines Support for both real-time and batch prediction patterns Cloud-native design using Azure Machine Learning Business Architecture Story How business data becomes a production prediction service Architecture Overview A business team provides historical data. The data science pipeline prepares the data, trains the model, evaluates quality, and registers the approved model. The DevOps pipeline then deploys the model as an endpoint that applications or business systems can call for predictions. The architecture separates infrastructure, training, and deployment into independent Azure DevOps pipelines. Business data is registered in Azure ML, processed through a training pipeline, evaluated, versioned in the model registry, and then deployed through either real-time online endpoints or lower-cost batch inference depending on the business use case. 1 Business Data 2 Data Preparation 3 Model Training 4 Model Evaluation 5 Model Registry 6 Online / Batch Deployment 7 Prediction Service 8 Business Application Business Data | v Azure DevOps Repository | +--> Infrastructure Pipeline | | | v | Azure Resource Group | | | v | Azure ML Workspace | | | v | Azure ML Compute Cluster | +--> Training Pipeline | v Register Data Asset | v Data Preparation | v Model Training | v Model Evaluation | v Azure ML Model Registry | v Deployment Pipeline | +--> Online Endpoint -> Real-Time Prediction API | +--> Batch Endpoint -> Offline Batch Predictions | v Business Applications / Reports | v Business Decisions | v Monitoring, Feedback & Retraining Detailed Project Modules Technical deep-dive pages linked from the business-first overview Platform Foundation (IaC - Azure ML) Infrastructure-as-code, Azure ML workspace setup, compute foundation, and Azure DevOps integration. Open module 01 ML Training Pipeline Dataset registration, data preparation, training, evaluation, and model registration workflow. Open module 02 Production Deployment & Ops Online deployment, endpoint testing, and operational deployment patterns for production ML. Open module 03 Cost-Aware Azure MLOps Architecture FinOps thinking built into training, deployment, and infrastructure choices Cost-Aware Design This project was designed with cloud cost control in mind. The architecture separates training, deployment, and infrastructure pipelines so compute resources are only used when required, while production endpoints can be managed based on real business demand. The training compute cluster uses a scale-to-zero configuration, meaning no training compute cost is incurred when the pipeline is idle. This makes the solution suitable for experimentation, scheduled retraining, and cost-controlled proof-of-concept deployments. For production inference, the project supports both online and batch deployment patterns. Online endpoints are suitable for real-time API predictions but have a continuous running cost. Batch endpoints are more cost-effective for scheduled or offline scoring workloads where real-time response is not required. Cost Considerations Implemented Azure ML training compute configured with minimum nodes set to zero Training cluster scales up only during pipeline execution Dedicated online endpoint used only when real-time prediction is required Batch endpoint structure included for lower-cost offline inference Separate infrastructure, training, and deployment pipelines for better cost visibility Workspace uses Blob Storage, Key Vault, Container Registry, and Application Insights Azure DevOps pipelines can run within free-tier limits for small project usage Estimated Cost Profile Environment Type Approximate Monthly Cost Demo / portfolio setup with endpoint deleted after testing $5-$40 Occasional training with workspace and storage retained $10-$50 One always-on online endpoint $100-$180 Small production-style setup $200-$500 Multi-environment setup such as dev and prod $300-$700+ For low-volume or scheduled prediction workloads, batch inference can be used to reduce cost.

title: Enterprise AI Platform on Azure | End-to-End Azure MLOps Pipelines
content: Cost Considerations Implemented Azure ML training compute configured with minimum nodes set to zero Training cluster scales up only during pipeline execution Dedicated online endpoint used only when real-time prediction is required Batch endpoint structure included for lower-cost offline inference Separate infrastructure, training, and deployment pipelines for better cost visibility Workspace uses Blob Storage, Key Vault, Container Registry, and Application Insights Azure DevOps pipelines can run within free-tier limits for small project usage Estimated Cost Profile Environment Type Approximate Monthly Cost Demo / portfolio setup with endpoint deleted after testing $5-$40 Occasional training with workspace and storage retained $10-$50 One always-on online endpoint $100-$180 Small production-style setup $200-$500 Multi-environment setup such as dev and prod $300-$700+ For low-volume or scheduled prediction workloads, batch inference can be used to reduce cost. For real-time business applications, the online endpoint can be enabled with a clear understanding of the monthly compute cost. Resources the Code Uses Core services, resource settings, and main cost drivers Resource Inventory Azure Resource Group Azure ML Workspace in eastus Azure ML Compute Cluster: Standard_D2s_v3 Training cluster min nodes: 0 Training cluster max nodes: 4 Azure ML Online Endpoint Online deployment instance: STANDARD_D2AS_V4 Instance count: 1 Azure ML Model Registry Blob Storage, Key Vault, Container Registry, Application Insights Azure DevOps pipelines Main Cost Drivers The biggest cost is the online endpoint because the deployment uses STANDARD_D2AS_V4 with instance_count: 1 . If this runs 24/7, estimate around $70/month for VM compute alone before storage, logs, registry, and workspace support services. Training compute is cost-controlled because the cluster is configured as training_target_min_nodes: 0 , training_target_max_nodes: 4 , and training_target_sku: Standard_D2s_v3 . That means the training cluster can scale down to zero when idle. Estimated Monthly Cost Scenario Estimated Cost Infrastructure created, no training, no endpoint running $5-$25/month Occasional training only $10-$40/month Training + one always-on online endpoint $85-$130/month Training + endpoint + logs/storage growing $100-$180/month If scaled to 2 endpoint instances $160-$260/month Dev + prod environments $180-$350+/month For a portfolio/demo project, keep the endpoint deleted when not testing. Then the monthly cost should stay around $5-$40. For a small production setup, a realistic range is often $200-$500/month depending on traffic, uptime, monitoring, and retraining frequency.

(No ML models or training pipelines are implemented in Pipeline‑1; those are handled in Pipelines 2 & 3.) CI/CD , containerisation or orchestration tools used GitHub Actions – CI/CD orchestration AWS CDK (Python) – Infrastructure pipeline as code OIDC (GitHub → AWS) – Secure CI/CD authentication AWS CloudFormation – IaC execution backend (via CDK synth/deploy) Docker – Asset bundling for CDK (where applicable) (No Kubernetes/EKS or runtime container orchestration in Pipeline‑1; orchestration is infra provisioning only.) Monitoring, Logging, or optimisation practices CI/CD Observability: GitHub Actions logs for build/deploy visibility and failure diagnosis. IaC Execution Logs: CloudFormation stack events for deployment status and rollback tracing. Security Auditing: IAM policy scoping and change tracking via CDK diffs. Cost Optimisation Guardrails: Instance defaults + AWS Budgets and quota checks to prevent overspend. Skills & technologies Primary (advanced) AWS CDK (Python) AWS IAM, OIDC, KMS, least‑privilege SageMaker platform setup (Domain, Org Templates) GitHub Actions CI/CD CloudFormation / IaC Secondary tools S3 · SSM Parameter Store · KMS Docker (CDK asset bundling) Node.js / npm · Python virtualenv Git, YAML, Bash Cloud & DevOps AWS (SageMaker, IAM, KMS, S3, SSM, CloudFormation) AWS CDK GitHub Actions OIDC Service Catalog Challenges & Outcomes Key technical challenges faced: Designing reusable SageMaker Organization Templates that work across multiple environments and teams. Secure CI/CD authentication between GitHub Actions and AWS using OIDC without long-lived credentials. IAM least-privilege policy design for CDK deployments and Service Catalog products (avoiding over-permissioning). Service Quota constraints for SageMaker instance types during initial provisioning. Config management across environments (Dev/Pre-Prod/Prod) without hardcoding account/region values. How they were resolved: Modular CDK stacks & constructs were used to make SageMaker Org Templates reusable and environment-agnostic. OIDC-based IAM roles were configured for GitHub Actions to enable secure, keyless CI/CD authentication. Least-privilege IAM policies were iteratively refined using CDK diff and deployment logs to scope permissions correctly. Service quota planning was documented and pre-approved to avoid provisioning failures during scale-up. Centralized configuration via SSM Parameter Store + env variables removed hardcoded values and enabled clean multi-env deployments. CI/CD Architecture & YAML mapping Architecture Block AWS CI/CD Construct Source Repository GitHub (IaC repo) Source Trigger GitHub Actions trigger (push / workflow_dispatch) CI Runner GitHub Actions Linux Runner (ubuntu-latest) Build / IaC Execution AWS CDK (cdk bootstrap / synth / deploy) Infrastructure Backend AWS CloudFormation (via CDK synth) Artifact Storage Amazon S3 (CDK assets / templates) Platform Provisioning Target Amazon SageMaker (Domain, Org Templates via Service Catalog) Template Registry AWS Service Catalog (Org Templates as Products) Security & Auth OIDC (GitHub → AWS) + IAM Roles & Policies Secrets / Config AWS SSM Parameter Store (+ env vars) Encryption AWS KMS (platform resources) Logs & Monitoring GitHub Actions Logs + CloudFormation Stack Events AWS DevOps CI/CD – Reference Architecture Pipeline-1: IaC / Platform Provisioning Architecture Flow: Developer pushes changes to GitHub (IaC repository). GitHub Actions workflow is triggered (push / manual dispatch). GitHub Actions assumes AWS IAM Role via OIDC (no static credentials). CDK bootstrap initializes target AWS account/region (one-time). CDK synth generates CloudFormation templates. CDK deploy provisions: SageMaker Domain & Organization Templates (Service Catalog Product) IAM Roles & Policies KMS Keys S3 Buckets (CDK assets) CloudFormation executes stacks with rollback on failure. Logs and status are available in GitHub Actions + CloudFormation events.

GitHub Actions workflow is triggered (push / manual dispatch). GitHub Actions assumes AWS IAM Role via OIDC (no static credentials). CI job packages pipeline code and dependencies. title: Enterprise AI Platform on AWS · SageMaker MLOps Pipelines
content: Title: Enterprise AI Platform on AWS · SageMaker MLOps Pipelines Source URL: https://rajesharigala.com/ai-work/ai-work3 Headings: H1: Enterprise AI Platform on AWS H2: End-to-End AWS SageMaker MLOps H2: Business Problem H2: What The Project Does H2: Business Use Cases H2: Business Architecture Story H2: Complete Architecture Diagram H2: Cost-Aware AWS MLOps (FinOps) H2: Resources Used H3: End-to-End AWS SageMaker MLOps Pipelines for Production ML Deployment H3: Secure CI/CD H3: Governed promotion H3: Automation first H3: The pain H3: The solution H3: Enterprise SageMaker Foundation H3: SageMaker Pipeline Process→Train→Evaluate→Register H3: Registry-Driven Serving Approved→Endpoint H3: Key capabilities H3: Business value H3: Cross-industry applications H3: Plain-text flow H3: Key cost controls H3: Estimated monthly cost profile H3: Core stack H3: Supporting services Paragraphs: Enterprise AI Platform on AWS: a complete, enterprise‑grade MLOps foundation built with Amazon SageMaker, AWS CDK (IaC), GitHub Actions, and OIDC authentication. It automates infrastructure provisioning, model training with quality gates, governed registry approvals, and production endpoint deployment across Dev / Pre‑Prod / Prod. The demo use case is Abalone Age Prediction (regression). The real value is the reusable platform pattern—portable to demand forecasting, pricing, churn, risk scoring, and predictive maintenance. This project demonstrates a complete enterprise‑grade MLOps platform on AWS using Amazon SageMaker, AWS CDK (Infrastructure as Code), GitHub Actions, and OIDC authentication. It automates the ML lifecycle across infrastructure, training with quality gates, and registry‑driven production deployment. OIDC‑based GitHub Actions avoids static cloud credentials. Deployments are scoped with least‑privilege IAM and can be cleanly separated per environment. Models are versioned in SageMaker Model Registry and promoted only after manual approval gates to reduce risk. Infrastructure, training, evaluation, registration, and deployment are automated so teams can iterate quickly with traceability. Notebook models are easy. Production ML is hard without a repeatable delivery platform. Many teams train models manually and deploy inconsistently. They lack governance, monitoring, and secure automation—leading to delays, quality issues, security risks, and high operational overhead. This project delivers a reusable AWS MLOps foundation that standardizes training, evaluation, registry governance, and production deployment using native AWS services. The platform is built as three modular pipelines. AWS CDK · Service Catalog · Organization Templates · SageMaker Domain XGBoost · quality gates · Model Registry · manual approval AWS CDK · registry promotion · real-time + batch · multi-env Abalone Age Prediction is the demo. The same architecture powers real enterprise workloads. A business team provides historical data. The MLOps platform handles preparation, training, evaluation with quality gates, governed registration, and automated promotion to scalable production endpoints for applications to consume. Designed with FinOps principles: scale-to-zero jobs, on-demand endpoints, and quality gates to prevent wasteful deployments. Core AWS services and compute profiles that power the platform. Ready to deploy an enterprise AI platform on AWS? Let's build automated infrastructure, training pipelines, and production-grade serving with full governance and cost optimisation. Main Content: End-to-End AWS SageMaker MLOps This project demonstrates a complete enterprise‑grade MLOps platform on AWS using Amazon SageMaker, AWS CDK (Infrastructure as Code), GitHub Actions, and OIDC authentication. It automates the ML lifecycle across infrastructure, training with quality gates, and registry‑driven production deployment. Secure CI/CD OIDC‑based GitHub Actions avoids static cloud credentials. Deployments are scoped with least‑privilege IAM and can be cleanly separated per environment. Governed promotion Models are versioned in SageMaker Model Registry and promoted only after manual approval gates to reduce risk. Automation first Infrastructure, training, evaluation, registration, and deployment are automated so teams can iterate quickly with traceability. Business Problem Notebook models are easy. Production ML is hard without a repeatable delivery platform. The pain Many teams train models manually and deploy inconsistently. They lack governance, monitoring, and secure automation—leading to delays, quality issues, security risks, and high operational overhead. Impact: slow releases, fragile deployments, limited auditability, and expensive rework. The solution This project delivers a reusable AWS MLOps foundation that standardizes training, evaluation, registry governance, and production deployment using native AWS services. Outcome: consistent, reproducible, and governed ML operations across environments.

title: Enterprise AI Platform on AWS · SageMaker MLOps Pipelines
content: Automation first Infrastructure, training, evaluation, registration, and deployment are automated so teams can iterate quickly with traceability. Business Problem Notebook models are easy. Production ML is hard without a repeatable delivery platform. The pain Many teams train models manually and deploy inconsistently. They lack governance, monitoring, and secure automation—leading to delays, quality issues, security risks, and high operational overhead. Impact: slow releases, fragile deployments, limited auditability, and expensive rework. The solution This project delivers a reusable AWS MLOps foundation that standardizes training, evaluation, registry governance, and production deployment using native AWS services. Outcome: consistent, reproducible, and governed ML operations across environments. What The Project Does The platform is built as three modular pipelines. Pipeline‑1 · Infrastructure (IaC) Enterprise SageMaker Foundation AWS CDK · Service Catalog · Organization Templates · SageMaker Domain SageMaker Domain + Studio provisioning Organization Templates (Service Catalog) IAM least‑privilege, KMS, S3 artifacts GitHub Actions OIDC + CDK bootstrap/synth/deploy View IaC details Pipeline‑2 · Training SageMaker Pipeline Process→Train→Evaluate→Register XGBoost · quality gates · Model Registry · manual approval Automated preprocessing, training, evaluation MSE quality gate – conditional registration Model Registry + manual approval stage GitHub Actions + OIDC orchestration View training pipeline Pipeline‑3 · Deployment Registry-Driven Serving Approved→Endpoint AWS CDK · registry promotion · real-time + batch · multi-env Registry‑driven model promotion (latest Approved) SageMaker Model + EndpointConfig + Endpoint YAML instance config, traffic weights GitHub Actions CDK deploy + OIDC View deployment details Key capabilities Infrastructure as Code with AWS CDK (Python) Secure CI/CD using GitHub Actions + OIDC (no static credentials) Quality gates during training evaluation Model governance with SageMaker Model Registry + manual approval Multi‑environment support (Dev / Pre‑Prod / Prod) Support for both real‑time and batch inference Business value Faster time from model development to production Consistent, reproducible, and auditable ML processes Strong governance and traceability via Model Registry Reduced manual effort and operational risk Clear separation of infrastructure, training, and deployment Secure, scalable, cost‑aware design using AWS services Business Use Cases Abalone Age Prediction is the demo. The same architecture powers real enterprise workloads. Cross-industry applications Transportation: fare prediction, ETA, demand forecasting, route optimization Retail: sales forecasting, dynamic pricing, inventory optimization Finance: credit risk scoring, fraud detection, customer churn prediction Healthcare: patient risk scoring, readmission prediction Manufacturing: predictive maintenance, quality control, yield optimization Marketing: customer lifetime value, next-best-action recommendation Business Architecture Story A business team provides historical data. The MLOps platform handles preparation, training, evaluation with quality gates, governed registration, and automated promotion to scalable production endpoints for applications to consume. Plain-text flow Business Data → GitHub (IaC + Seed Code) → Pipeline‑1: Infrastructure (CDK + Service Catalog) → SageMaker Domain + Templates → Pipeline‑2: Training (SageMaker Pipelines) → Process → Train → Evaluate → Model Registry → Pipeline‑3: Deployment (CDK) → Approved Model → Real-time Endpoint / Batch → Business Applications → Predictions → Decisions + Monitoring + Retraining 1 Business Data 2 GitHub + OIDC 3 IaC (Pipeline‑1) 4 Train + Gates 5 Registry Approval 6 Deploy + Monitor Complete Architecture Diagram GitHub Actions OIDC + IAM CDK (IaC) SageMaker Endpoints End-to-end automation: CDK provisions enterprise-ready SageMaker infrastructure, pipelines train with quality gates, approved models promote to real-time or batch endpoints—governed by OIDC and least-privilege IAM. Cost-Aware AWS MLOps (FinOps) Designed with FinOps principles: scale-to-zero jobs, on-demand endpoints, and quality gates to prevent wasteful deployments. Key cost controls Scale-to-zero training and processing jobs On-demand SageMaker endpoints (easy to create/delete) Infrastructure as Code for quick spin-up/teardown Quality gates to prevent wasteful deployments Batch Transform for non-urgent workloads Main cost driver: real-time SageMaker endpoints. Delete endpoints after testing for portfolio/demo projects to stay under ~$60/month. Estimated monthly cost profile Scenario Description Approx. monthly cost Portfolio / Demo No running endpoint $15 – $60 Active Development Occasional training + testing $50 – $150 Production Pilot One real-time endpoint + retraining $120 – $280 Small Production Endpoint + monitoring + weekly retraining $200 – $500 Recommendation: use Batch Transform where latency is not critical; keep real-time endpoints only when the business requires low-latency predictions.

content: Key cost controls Scale-to-zero training and processing jobs On-demand SageMaker endpoints (easy to create/delete) Infrastructure as Code for quick spin-up/teardown Quality gates to prevent wasteful deployments Batch Transform for non-urgent workloads Main cost driver: real-time SageMaker endpoints. Resources Used Core AWS services and compute profiles that power the platform. Core stack AWS CDK (Python) + GitHub Actions + OIDC Amazon SageMaker (Domain, Pipelines, Model Registry, Endpoints) Training / Processing: ml.m5.xlarge (scale-to-zero) Real-time Endpoint: ml.m5.xlarge Supporting services S3, ECR, CloudWatch KMS, SSM Parameter Store AWS Service Catalog (Organization Templates) Multi-environment support (Dev / Pre-Prod / Prod)

title: AI‑GCP Pipeline‑1 : Agent Platform Platform Foundation · Case Study
content: Title: AI‑GCP Pipeline‑1 : Agent Platform Platform Foundation · Case Study Source URL: https://rajesharigala.com/mlops/ai4/ai4.1 Headings: H1: Agent Platform Foundation H2: Project Summary H2: Key technologies & concepts H2: Problem & Objective H2: Solution & Architecture H2: Skills & Technologies H2: GCP DevOps CI/CD · Architecture & YAML Mapping H2: Complete Project Details H2: Challenges & Outcomes H2: Assets & References H3: Category H3: Domain H3: Focus H3: Problems solved H3: Primary objective H3: Platform foundation design H3: Key components (GCP) H3: Handshake: Google Colab ↔ Google Cloud H3: Primary skills H3: Secondary tools H3: Project Summary H3: Key Words H3: Problem Solved H3: Solution & Architecture H3: AI / DevOps Details H3: Monitoring & Optimization H3: Skills & Technologies Used H3: Challenges & Resolutions H3: GCP Production‑Grade Implementation Details H3: Assets & References H3: Study Material H3: Pipeline‑1 Summary H3: Key challenges H3: Resolutions H3: Repository H3: Notebook H3: Weblink H3: Proof Link H3: Study material resources H3: Agent Platform platform study material Paragraphs: Programmatic MLOps infrastructure on GCP Project: AI‑GCP Pipeline‑1 · Provisioning a production‑ready GCP AI platform for Agent Platform pipelines using programmatic IAM, GCS artifact storage, and SDK‑driven MLOps bootstrap. Cloud + MLOps AI platform engineering Kubeflow Pipelines v2 Agent Platform foundation Cloud + MLOps · AI Platform Infrastructure AI Platform Engineering / MLOps (GCP Agent Platform) MLOps Platform Engineering · infrastructure as code GCP native MLOps stack Why this platform bootstrap? Programmatic Agent Platform bootstrap Programmatic bootstrap configures GCP project, enables Agent Platform APIs, provisions IAM service accounts (least‑privilege), creates GCS artifact root, and sets up Agent Platform Pipelines runtime context — all through SDK + config, eliminating console drift. Infrastructure automation only (no models). Pipeline‑1 lays the secure, reproducible base for training & deployment pipelines. Pipeline‑1 includes the secure handshake between Google Colab and Google Cloud so notebooks can initialize the same Agent Platform project, region, service account, and GCS pipeline root used by the production pipeline runtime. MLOps platform expertise Pipeline‑1 platform bootstrap constructs Pipeline‑1 is the enterprise-grade GCP platform bootstrap: GitHub Actions + Workload Identity Federation securely provision Agent Platform Pipelines runtime, IAM service accounts, GCS artifact stores, and governance foundations for downstream AI workflows. All content from the Pipeline‑1 PDF Manual and ad‑hoc setup of GCP AI infrastructure leads to inconsistent Agent Platform environments, misconfigured IAM/service accounts, insecure artifact access, and non‑reproducible ML pipelines. This creates fragile MLOps workflows, operational drift across environments, and difficulty scaling ML workloads reliably across teams. Establish a secure, reproducible GCP AI platform foundation by programmatically bootstrapping project context, IAM service accounts, artifact storage (GCS), and pipeline runtime configuration—enabling consistent, governed execution of MLOps pipelines across environments without console‑driven dependencies. The solution implements a programmatic Agent Platform bootstrap that configures the GCP project context, initializes services, provisions and wires GCS artifact storage, and sets up IAM service accounts with least‑privilege access for pipeline execution. This creates the standardized AI platform foundation on GCP on top of which Pipeline‑2 (Train/Evaluate/Register) and Pipeline‑3 (Deploy/Serve/Schedule) run. Pipeline‑1 provisions the AI platform foundation on GCP, including project context initialization, IAM service accounts for secure pipeline execution, GCS artifact storage for datasets/models, and Agent Platform Pipelines runtime configuration. This platform layer is the standardized base for Pipeline‑2 and Pipeline‑3. Enterprise‑grade platform bootstrap on Google Cloud using GitHub Actions + Workload Identity Federation to securely provision Agent Platform Pipelines runtime, IAM service accounts, GCS artifact stores, and governance foundations for production ML pipelines. This layer standardizes security, storage, lineage, and execution context for all downstream AI workflows. Technical resolutions Code, diagrams, study material GCP Agent Platform pipeline code and deployment definitions. KFP v2 notebook for Agent Platform pipeline implementation. Published project page for Pipeline‑1. Proof link placeholder from the project brief. Complete Project Details All content from the Pipeline‑1 PDF Project Summary Project Name: AI‑GCP Pipeline‑1: Agent Platform Foundation (Programmatic MLOps Infrastructure) One‑Line Description: Provisioning a production‑ready GCP AI platform foundation for Agent Platform pipelines using programmatic IAM, GCS artifact storage, and SDK‑driven MLOps bootstrap. Category: Cloud + MLOps (AI Platform / Infrastructure Foundation) Industry: Cross‑Industry (Enterprise AI Platforms / Cloud Infrastructure) Domain: AI Platform Engineering / MLOps Infrastructure (GCP Agent Platform) Key Words Google Agent Platform (AI Platform Foundation) Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP v2 Runtime for ML Orchestration) Google Cloud IAM (Service Accounts & Least‑Privilege Policies) Google Cloud Storage (GCS Artifact Store for Pipelines) Agent Platform SDK (Programmatic Platform Bootstrap) Google Cloud Projects & APIs (Agent Platform Enablement) Programmatic Infrastructure Provisioning (SDK + gcloud) ML Platform Bootstrapping (Pipeline Runtime Setup) Multi‑Environment Platform Setup (Dev / Pre‑Prod / Prod via Config) Pipeline as Code (KFP v2 Pipeline Specs) Agent Platform Pipelines Runtime (Managed ML Orchestration) Service Account Impersonation (Secure Pipeline Execution) Cloud Logging & Monitoring (Agent Platform / Cloud Logging) Artifact Lineage & Storage (GCS + Agent Platform Metadata) Cost‑Aware Platform Defaults (Region, Machine Types, Quotas) Problem Solved Manual and ad‑hoc setup of GCP AI infrastructure leads to inconsistent Agent Platform environments, misconfigured IAM/service accounts, insecure artifact access, and non‑reproducible ML pipelines. This creates fragile MLOps workflows, operational drift across environments, and difficulty scaling ML workloads reliably across teams. Primary Objective Establish a secure, reproducible GCP AI platform foundation by programmatically bootstrapping project context, IAM service accounts, artifact storage (GCS), and pipeline runtime configuration—enabling consistent, governed execution of MLOps pipelines across environments without console‑driven dependencies. Solution & Architecture The solution implements a programmatic Agent Platform bootstrap that configures the GCP project context, initializes services, provisions and wires GCS artifact storage, and sets up IAM service accounts with least‑privilege access for pipeline execution. This creates the standardized AI platform foundation on GCP on top of which Pipeline‑2 (Train/Evaluate/Register) and Pipeline‑3 (Deploy/Serve/Schedule) run.

No structured way to enforce model quality before deployment in GCP. Build a production‑grade, automated ML training/evaluation pipeline on GCP that enforces quality gates, tracks lineage, and conditionally deploys models to Agent Platform endpoints using native MLOps primitives. Agent Platform native orchestration Agent Platform Pipelines (KFP v2) orchestrates data preparation, model training (RandomForest), evaluation (ROC, confusion matrix, accuracy), quality gating, conditional deployment to Agent Platform Endpoints, and scheduled retraining. Our platform automates machine learning workflows using Components as modular building blocks for specific tasks. These are orchestrated via a DSL (Domain Specific Language), which serves as the instruction manual for connecting them, while Conditions provide the "if‑then" logic to ensure the pipeline makes smart, real‑time decisions during execution. ML/platform engineering stack Conditional gates, lineage, scheduling Wiring KFP v2 components → Agent Platform Pipelines: used native KFP interfaces. ROC/metrics logging: sanitized inputs for Agent Platform metrics APIs. Conditional gates: pipeline condition with threshold check. Model format for serving: packaged as Agent Platform-compatible artifact. Notebook to production: refactored into pipeline components. Pipeline‑2 model training, evaluation, and governance constructs Pipeline‑2 standardizes reproducible training workflows, centralized GCS artifacts, metric logging (ROC, confusion matrix, accuracy), and governed model versioning for controlled promotion toward deployment. All content from the Pipeline‑2 PDF Manual, notebook‑driven ML workflows lack reproducibility, governance, automated evaluation gates, and production deployment discipline. There was no structured way to enforce model quality before deployment in GCP. Build a production‑grade, automated ML training and evaluation pipeline on GCP that enforces quality gates, tracks lineage, and conditionally deploys models to Agent Platform endpoints using platform‑native MLOps primitives. Implemented an Agent Platform Pipelines (KFP v2) based ML pipeline that performs data preparation, model training, evaluation (ROC, confusion matrix, accuracy), quality gating, conditional deployment to Agent Platform Endpoints, and scheduled retraining. Our platform automates machine learning workflows using Components as modular building blocks for specific tasks. These are orchestrated via a DSL (Domain Specific Language), which serves as the instruction manual for connecting them, while Conditions provide the if‑then logic to ensure smart, real‑time decisions during execution. Architecture: Agent Platform Pipelines → Training → Evaluation → Conditional Deployment → Endpoints; artifact persistence in GCS; lineage in Agent Platform Metadata Store. Production‑grade model development and orchestration on Google Cloud using Agent Platform Pipelines (KFP v2) to automate data preparation, model training, evaluation, and quality gates. This layer standardizes reproducible training workflows, centralized artifact storage in GCS, metric logging into experiments, and governed model versioning via the Model Registry, enabling controlled promotion of validated models toward deployment.

Online inference with managed Agent Platform Endpoints. AI + MLOps Model Serving Blue/Green Deployment Agent Platform Scheduler Model deployment & serving platform AI + MLOps · Cloud Platform Engineering Model Deployment Engineering / AI Serving Platforms Production online inference, endpoint management, retraining loops Agent Platform serving stack Why this deployment pipeline? Deployment + retraining loop Programmatic model upload to Agent Platform Model Registry, endpoint creation/reuse, traffic‑split deployment for new versions, and scheduled pipeline runs for continuous retraining & redeployment. All managed via Agent Platform SDK + KFP. Autoscaling endpoints, versioned rollbacks, canary deployments — fully managed on GCP. Model serving automation Model Serving + Deployment Automation (MLOps – Online Inference) MLOps serving expertise Pipeline‑3 (deployment) constructs Enterprise‑scale model serving with traffic splitting, canary rollouts, and cron‑based retraining. All content from the Pipeline‑3 PDF Deploying ML models to production endpoints reliably on GCP requires handling model packaging, endpoint lifecycle, traffic management, and scheduled retraining. Manual deployment introduces risk, drift, and inconsistency. Build a repeatable, production‑grade deployment and serving pipeline on Agent Platform with managed endpoints, versioned models, automated rollout, and scheduled pipeline execution for continuous model refresh. Implemented an Agent Platform deployment pipeline that programmatically uploads trained models, provisions or reuses endpoints, deploys model versions with controlled traffic, and schedules periodic pipeline runs for continuous retraining and redeployment. Architecture implemented on GCP: Trained models → Agent Platform Model Registry → Endpoint Deployment → Online Inference → Scheduled Pipeline Re‑runs. title: AI‑GCP Pipeline‑3 : Agent Platform Deployment & Endpoints · Case Study
content: Category: AI + MLOps + Cloud Platform Engineering Industry: Cross‑industry (Enterprise AI Platform / MLOps Infrastructure) Domain: Model Deployment Engineering / AI Serving Platforms Key Words Agent Platform Model Upload (Model Registry Equivalent) Agent Platform Endpoints (Managed Online Inference) Model Versioning & Traffic Splitting Online Prediction APIs Agent Platform Pipeline Scheduling Blue/Green or Canary Deployment (Traffic Split) Artifact Registry (Serving Containers) GCS Model Artifacts Service Accounts & IAM (Secure Serving Identity) Cloud Logging & Monitoring (Inference Logs) Endpoint Lifecycle Management Production AI Serving Architecture Problem Solved Deploying ML models to production endpoints reliably on GCP requires handling model packaging, endpoint lifecycle, traffic management, and scheduled retraining. Manual deployment introduces risk, drift, and inconsistency. Primary Objective Build a repeatable, production‑grade deployment and serving pipeline on Agent Platform with managed endpoints, versioned models, automated rollout, and scheduled pipeline execution for continuous model refresh. Solution & Architecture Implemented an Agent Platform deployment pipeline that programmatically uploads trained models, provisions or reuses endpoints, deploys model versions with controlled traffic, and schedules periodic pipeline runs for continuous retraining and redeployment. Cloud Platform: Google Cloud Platform (Agent Platform) Key components: Agent Platform Model Registry (Model upload), Agent Platform Endpoints (Online inference), Agent Platform Pipelines Scheduler (Recurrent jobs), GCS (Model artifact storage), Artifact Registry (Serving container images), IAM Service Accounts (Secure serving identity), Cloud Logging & Monitoring (Endpoint observability) Scalability / Reliability: Managed Agent Platform Endpoints with autoscaling; traffic splitting for safe rollouts (canary/blue‑green); versioned deployments with rollback capability; scheduled retraining pipelines to prevent model drift; stateless serving with durable artifact storage in GCS AI / DevOps Details AI/ML type or DevOps focus: Model Serving + Deployment Automation (MLOps – Online Inference) Models, pipeline, or automation: model upload automation to Agent Platform; endpoint creation & reuse logic; traffic‑split deployment for new versions; pipeline scheduling for continuous retraining; online prediction API invocation CI/CD, containerisation or orchestration tools: GitHub Actions (CI trigger), Agent Platform Pipelines (orchestration), Artifact Registry (serving containers), Agent Platform Endpoints (serving runtime) Monitoring, Logging & Optimization Cloud Logging for inference requests Agent Platform Endpoint metrics (latency, throughput) Traffic shifting for rollout safety Scheduled pipeline runs for drift mitigation Skills & Technologies Used Primary Skills: AI Deployment Architecture (Advanced), Agent Platform Endpoint Engineering (Advanced), MLOps Production Deployment (Advanced), Cloud AI Platform Engineering (Advanced) Secondary Tools / Frameworks: Agent Platform SDK (Python), Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP v2), scikit‑learn (model framework), Google Cloud Storage Programming Language: Python (primary), YAML (configuration / pipeline specs where applicable) Cloud & DevOps Tools: Google Agent Platform, Artifact Registry, GCS, IAM / Service Accounts, GitHub Actions, Agent Platform Pipeline Scheduler Challenges & Outcomes Packaging models to match Agent Platform serving container requirements: resolved with standardized model artifact layout for Agent Platform containers Managing endpoint lifecycle (create vs reuse): resolved with programmatic endpoint discovery & reuse Safe rollout of new model versions: resolved with traffic‑split based deployment strategy Operationalizing scheduled retraining on GCP: resolved with Agent Platform Pipeline scheduling with controlled concurrency GCP Production‑Grade Implementation Details Architecture implemented on GCP: Trained models → Agent Platform Model Registry → Endpoint Deployment → Online Inference → Scheduled Pipeline Re‑runs. Top lane — Online Serving Path: Trained Model (from Pipeline‑2) → Agent Platform Model Upload (Model Registry) → Agent Platform Endpoint (Create / Reuse) → Online Inference API (Real‑Time Predictions) Bottom lane — Monitoring & Retraining Loop: Online Inference API / Endpoint → Monitoring & Logs (Cloud Logging, Metrics) → Scheduled Retraining (Agent Platform Pipeline Scheduler) → Back to Model Upload (Model Registry) Process Flow: Model Artifact (GCS) → Agent Platform Model Upload → Endpoint Provisioning / Reuse → Traffic‑Split Deployment → Online Inference API → Monitoring & Logs → Scheduled Pipeline Triggers The main project document has detailed view. title: AI GCP AUTOMATION PROJECT · End-to-End Enterprise AI Platform on GCP
content: Title: AI GCP AUTOMATION PROJECT · End-to-End Enterprise AI Platform on GCP Source URL: https://rajesharigala.com/ai-work/ai-work4 Headings: H1: Enterprise AI Platform on GCP H2: Business Problem H2: What The Project Does H2: Business Architecture Story H2: Complete Architecture Diagram H2: Cost-Aware GCP MLOps (FinOps) H2: Resources Used H3: End-to-End Vertex AI MLOps Pipelines for Production ML Deployment H3: Why teams get stuck H3: What this project solves H3: Colab → GCS Foundation H3: Process → Train → Evaluate Quality Gates H3: Approved → Endpoint Batch Support H3: Key capabilities H3: Business value H3: End-to-End Flow H3: Cost controls implemented H3: Estimated monthly cost profile H3: GCP services H3: Developer workflow Paragraphs: Enterprise-grade MLOps platform on Google Cloud: built with Vertex AI, GitHub, and Google Colab. It automates the full ML lifecycle across modular pipelines — from experimentation to governed registry promotion and production deployment — with strong automation and cost control. The demo use case is Wine Quality Prediction (regression/classification). The real value is the reusable platform pattern—adaptable for demand forecasting, pricing, churn, risk scoring, and operational optimization. Notebook experiments are easy. Productionizing ML reliably—across teams and environments—is the challenge. Many teams build models in notebooks (Colab/Jupyter) but struggle to productionize them. Models are trained manually, deployed inconsistently, hard to monitor or retrain, and lack governance and version control. This project delivers a repeatable, automated, and governed MLOps platform on GCP using Vertex AI—turning raw datasets into scalable prediction services with controlled promotion and cost-aware execution. Three modular pipelines automate experimentation, training with quality gates, and registry-driven deployment. Google Colab · dataset storage/versioning · GitHub integration Scikit-learn / XGBoost / AutoML optional · registry-ready artifacts Registry-driven deployment · controlled rollout · versioning A business team provides structured data. The platform prepares data, trains and evaluates models with gates, registers approved models, then deploys to scalable endpoints for real-time or batch predictions. Managed services keep costs aligned with usage: no idle training compute, on-demand endpoints, and batch prediction for low-cost workloads. Services and building blocks that make the platform production-ready. ⚡ Production-grade, cloud-native AI platform on GCP — end-to-end MLOps system design, platform engineering capability, business-aligned deployment, FinOps awareness, and scalable governed infrastructure built on Vertex AI. Ready to deploy an enterprise AI platform on Google Cloud? Let's build secure Vertex AI infrastructure, automated training pipelines, and production-grade serving with closed-loop MLOps. Main Content: Business Problem Notebook experiments are easy. Productionizing ML reliably—across teams and environments—is the challenge. Why teams get stuck Many teams build models in notebooks (Colab/Jupyter) but struggle to productionize them. Models are trained manually, deployed inconsistently, hard to monitor or retrain, and lack governance and version control. Impact: delays, unreliable predictions, and operational inefficiencies. What this project solves This project delivers a repeatable, automated, and governed MLOps platform on GCP using Vertex AI—turning raw datasets into scalable prediction services with controlled promotion and cost-aware execution. Outcome: faster iteration with traceability, governance, and production readiness. What The Project Does Three modular pipelines automate experimentation, training with quality gates, and registry-driven deployment. Pipeline‑1 · Experimentation + Data Colab → GCS Foundation Google Colab · dataset storage/versioning · GitHub integration Data exploration and preprocessing in Google Colab Dataset storage and versioning in Cloud Storage Code integration and reuse via GitHub Clean handoff into Vertex AI training pipelines View IaC details Pipeline‑2 · Training (Vertex AI Pipelines) Process → Train → Evaluate Quality Gates Scikit-learn / XGBoost / AutoML optional · registry-ready artifacts Data preprocessing + feature engineering Model training and evaluation with quality gates Conditional model registration in Vertex AI Model Registry Versioned artifacts for reproducible promotion View training pipeline Pipeline‑3 · Deployment (Vertex AI) Approved → Endpoint Batch Support Registry-driven deployment · controlled rollout · versioning Registry-driven deployment of approved models Real-time endpoint creation Batch prediction support for cost-sensitive workloads Controlled rollout and versioning View deployment details Key capabilities End-to-end pipeline automation using Vertex AI Pipelines Experimentation integrated via Google Colab CI/CD workflows using GitHub Model governance using Vertex AI Model Registry Quality gates before deployment Multi-environment readiness (Dev / Pre-Prod / Prod) Real-time and batch inference support Serverless-first scalable architecture Business value Faster transition from experimentation to production Reproducible and version-controlled ML workflows Reduced manual effort and operational overhead Strong governance through model registry Scalable deployment architecture Clear separation of data/experimentation, training, and deployment Cost-efficient execution using managed cloud services Business Architecture Story A business team provides structured data.

The platform prepares data, trains and evaluates models with gates, registers approved models, then deploys to scalable endpoints for real-time or batch predictions. End-to-End Flow Business Data → GitHub Repository (Code + Pipelines) → Pipeline‑1: Experimentation (Colab + Cloud Storage) → Pipeline‑2: Training (Vertex AI Pipelines) → Process → Train → Evaluate → Model Registry → Pipeline‑3: Deployment (Vertex AI) → Approved Model → Endpoint / Batch Prediction → Business Apps → Predictions → Decisions → Monitoring → Retraining 1 Business Data 2 Colab + GCS 3 Vertex Pipelines 4 Model Registry 5 Endpoints / Batch 6 Monitor + Retrain Complete Architecture Diagram GitHub Colab GCS Vertex AI Endpoints Serverless-first design: Vertex AI manages compute automatically—training jobs provision on demand and terminate after completion; endpoints scale via replicas; batch prediction runs serverlessly with no persistent compute. Cost-Aware GCP MLOps (FinOps) Managed services keep costs aligned with usage: no idle training compute, on-demand endpoints, and batch prediction for low-cost workloads. Cost controls implemented Training jobs run only when triggered (auto-provision and terminate) Endpoints can be deployed and deleted on demand Batch prediction for cost-sensitive scheduled workloads Cloud Storage for low-cost persistence of datasets/artifacts GitHub-based workflows reduce infra overhead Main cost driver: Vertex AI real-time endpoints. For demos/portfolio, delete endpoints when not in use. Estimated monthly cost profile Environment type Approx monthly cost Portfolio / Demo (no endpoint running) $5 – $30 Experimentation + occasional training $10 – $60 One active real-time endpoint $80 – $180 Small production setup $150 – $400 Multi-environment (dev + prod) $250 – $600+ Recommendation: prefer batch inference for scheduled workloads, trigger training only when needed, and monitor usage with Cloud Monitoring. Resources Used Services and building blocks that make the platform production-ready. GCP services Vertex AI (Pipelines, Training, Model Registry, Endpoints) Cloud Storage (datasets and artifacts) Artifact Registry Cloud Logging & Monitoring Developer workflow Google Colab for experimentation GitHub for source control and CI/CD workflows Serverless-first managed compute via Vertex AI Multi-environment readiness (Dev / Pre-Prod / Prod) ⚡ Production-grade, cloud-native AI platform on GCP — end-to-end MLOps system design, platform engineering capability, business-aligned deployment, FinOps awareness, and scalable governed infrastructure built on Vertex AI.

MLOps / Platform Engineering AI + MLOps + Platform Engineering (Kubernetes / Kubeflow) Cross‑industry Enterprise AI Platforms / MLOps Infrastructure Model Training Engineering / Pipeline Orchestration Kubeflow native keywords Why this project exists Kubeflow pipeline flow Kubeflow Pipelines workflow orchestrates multiple containerized components for data ingestion, model training (Logistic Regression and Decision Tree), and evaluation. Each pipeline run produces versioned artifacts and metrics, enabling systematic comparison and model selection based on evaluation criteria. Automation, orchestration, and observability Model Training, Evaluation & Experiment Orchestration (MLOps – Offline Training). Proficiencies demonstrated Python (primary), YAML (pipeline/component specs). Reference: Pipeline‑2 modelling Pipeline‑2 implements Kubernetes‑native ML training and evaluation using Kubeflow Pipelines, providing the open-source equivalent of managed Vertex AI Pipelines for model experimentation, metric-based selection, and governed promotion to deployment workflows. Technical hurdles Code, diagrams, study material Official Kubeflow Pipelines repository and documentation source. Project repository for Kubeflow Pipelines MLOps implementation. Deployment link will be added later. Each pipeline run produces versioned artifacts and metrics, enabling systematic comparison and model selection based on evaluation criteria. title: Kubeflow ML Pipeline · MLOps case study
content: Flow: Data → Train (LR, DT) → Evaluate → Compare Metrics → (conditional selection) 1 Data ingestion 2 Train LR 3 Train DT 4 Evaluate 5 Compare / select Components Download data (component) Training (scikit‑learn containers) Evaluation metrics (accuracy) Kubeflow Pipelines orchestration MinIO artifact storage Platform & services Kubernetes + Kubeflow (KFP v2 runtime) Argo Workflows (execution engine) Docker (containerized training components) MinIO artifact storage for datasets and outputs Kubeflow Metadata Store (MLMD) for lineage and run metadata Kubernetes Pods, Services, Namespaces, RBAC Scalability & reliability Stateless pipeline components executed as isolated pods Retry and failure isolation at step level Artifact durability via object storage (MinIO) Re-runnable pipelines with deterministic environments via image versioning AI / DevOps Details Automation, orchestration, and observability AI/ML focus Model Training, Evaluation & Experiment Orchestration (MLOps – Offline Training). Pipeline automation Automated data ingestion component Parallel/serial training components (Logistic Regression, Decision Tree) Metric computation and logging (accuracy) Metric-based model comparison and selection Pipeline as code (compiled KFP YAML) CI/CD & orchestration Docker training component images Kubeflow Pipelines orchestration Argo Workflows pipeline execution Kubernetes runtime scheduling Monitoring & optimisation Kubeflow Pipelines UI for run status, DAG visualization, and step-level logs Kubernetes pod logs for component-level debugging ML Metadata Store for run lineage and artifact traceability Metric visibility (accuracy) across experiments for comparative analysis Failure isolation and targeted re-runs of failed steps Skills & Technologies Proficiencies demonstrated Primary skills Kubeflow Pipelines (advanced) Kubernetes‑Native MLOps (advanced) ML Training Pipeline Design (advanced) Experiment Tracking & Lineage (advanced) Secondary tools scikit‑learn (model framework) Docker (containerization) YAML (pipeline/component specifications) Argo Workflows MinIO, ML Metadata Kubeflow UI / SDK Docker Hub Programming languages Python (primary), YAML (pipeline/component specs). Kubeflow DevOps — Architecture & YAML mapping Reference: Pipeline‑2 modelling Architecture Block Kubeflow CI/CD / MLOps Construct (Pipeline‑2) Source Repository GitHub (Kubeflow modeling / pipelines repo) Source Trigger Manual trigger (Kubeflow UI / SDK) or CI trigger (GitHub Actions / local pipeline submission) CI Runner GitHub Actions Linux Runner (ubuntu-latest) (optional for CI-driven pipeline compilation and submission) Build / Pipeline Execution Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP v2: Data → Train → Evaluate → Condition) Training Orchestration Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP v2 running on Kubernetes via Argo Workflows) Data Processing Kubeflow Pipeline Component (Python + scikit‑learn preprocessing in containerized step) Model Evaluation Kubeflow Pipeline Component (Python + scikit‑learn evaluation component; accuracy metric) Artifact Storage MinIO (S3-compatible object store for datasets, model artifacts, metrics JSON) Container Registry Docker Hub (versioned training component images) Model Registry Kubeflow Pipelines run history + ML Metadata Store (MLMD) + MinIO artifact versions (open-source equivalent of managed model registries) Approval Gate Pipeline Condition (metric threshold gate for downstream deployment pipeline) Security & Auth Kubernetes Service Accounts + RBAC (least-privilege execution identity for pipeline pods) Secrets / Config Kubernetes Secrets + Environment Variables (mounted into pipeline containers) Monitoring & Logs Kubeflow Pipelines UI + Kubernetes Pod Logs (per-step execution logs) Lineage & Governance Kubeflow Pipelines lineage + ML Metadata Store (inputs/outputs, run lineage, artifact traceability) Infrastructure Backend Self-managed Kubernetes (Minikube / EKS / AKS / GKE) hosting Kubeflow Pipelines runtime (control plane + workflow engine deployed via Kubeflow manifests) Pipeline‑2 implements Kubernetes‑native ML training and evaluation using Kubeflow Pipelines, providing the open-source equivalent of managed Vertex AI Pipelines for model experimentation, metric-based selection, and governed promotion to deployment workflows. Challenges & Resolutions Technical hurdles Key challenges Packaging training code into reproducible container images Wiring artifact paths between components Consistent metric logging across variants Experiment lineage and traceability Resolutions Standardised Dockerfiles per component Kubeflow artifact inputs/outputs for clean handoffs Centralised accuracy logging for model comparison ML Metadata Store + KFP run history Assets & References Code, diagrams, study material Kubeflow Official Documentation Repo Official Kubeflow Pipelines repository and documentation source. kubeflow/pipelines Kubeflow Pipelines MLOps Project repository for Kubeflow Pipelines MLOps implementation. title: Enterprise Kubeflow MLOps Platform | Open‑Source Kubernetes AI
content: Title: Enterprise Kubeflow MLOps Platform | Open‑Source Kubernetes AI Source URL: https://rajesharigala.com/ai-work/ai-work5 Headings: H1: Enterprise AI Platform on Kubernetes H2: Three‑Phase Kubeflow MLOps H2: Business Problem & Solution H2: Business Architecture Story H2: Cost‑Aware Architecture: Kubeflow vs Managed ML H2: Resources & Technology Stack H2: Business Use Cases H3: Kubeflow Platform Foundation H3: Kubeflow Pipelines Train·Eval·Select H3: K8s Serving & Ops Serve·Monitor·Retrain* H3: The Challenge H3: The Kubeflow Solution H3: Managed Platforms vs Self‑Hosted Kubeflow H3: Estimated Monthly Cost (Self‑Hosted) H3: Scenario Breakdown H3: Storage & Metadata H3: Portability Paragraphs: End-to-End Kubeflow MLOps Pipelines for Production ML Deployment This project delivers a complete, enterprise-grade, Kubernetes-native MLOps platform using Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP v2). It automates the full machine learning lifecycle across three modular pipelines — from platform foundation to training, evaluation, model selection, and production serving — with strong emphasis on reproducibility, governance, and cost efficiency. Using the Breast Cancer Wisconsin Diagnostic dataset (binary classification), the platform demonstrates how organizations can build a scalable, self-managed MLOps stack. The real value lies in the reusable foundation — adaptable for fraud detection, churn prediction, risk scoring, and predictive maintenance. Modular pipelines for platform foundation, governed training, and production serving Kubernetes bootstrap · Kubeflow Pipelines · MinIO · RBAC · MLMD KFP v2 · containerized training · metrics · condition gate · MLMD K8s Deployments · KServe · canary rollouts · lineage Why enterprises need a repeatable, open‑source ML delivery platform Many organizations build models in notebooks but cannot productionize them consistently. Manual processes lead to environment drift, poor traceability, configuration inconsistencies, and high operational overhead. Without an MLOps platform, teams waste weeks on deployment and lack auditability for compliance. A fully reproducible, Kubernetes-native MLOps platform where infrastructure, training, and deployment are defined as code and executed reliably across environments. From business data to production predictions FinOps built into open‑source MLOps Open‑source tools powering the platform Reusable MLOps architecture across industries Real value is the reusable MLOps foundation: the same platform adapts to any prediction problem without changing infrastructure. Enterprise Kubeflow MLOps Platform · Open source, portable, cost‑aware · Built for production Complete end‑to‑end automation: infrastructure, training, evaluation, selection, and Kubernetes‑native serving Main Content: Enterprise AI Platform on Kubernetes End-to-End Kubeflow MLOps Pipelines for Production ML Deployment This project delivers a complete, enterprise-grade, Kubernetes-native MLOps platform using Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP v2). It automates the full machine learning lifecycle across three modular pipelines — from platform foundation to training, evaluation, model selection, and production serving — with strong emphasis on reproducibility, governance, and cost efficiency. Using the Breast Cancer Wisconsin Diagnostic dataset (binary classification), the platform demonstrates how organizations can build a scalable, self-managed MLOps stack. The real value lies in the reusable foundation — adaptable for fraud detection, churn prediction, risk scoring, and predictive maintenance.
